title: Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements
sources.claim: Pseudo-classes select state or structure such as :focus-visible, :checked, :disabled, and :nth-child, while pseudo-elements represent generated boxes.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements
sources.claim: Pseudo-classes select state or structure such as :focus-visible, :checked, :disabled, and :nth-child, while pseudo-elements represent generated boxes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Pseudo-classes select state or structure such as :focus-visible, :checked, :disabled, and :nth-child, while pseudo-elements represent generated boxes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A control has visible :focus-visible styling independent of its hover styling. Your goal is to implement hover, focus-visible, validation and structural states without hiding keyboard focus. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Specificity and source order; html semantics; Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors; html forms labels controls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Pseudo-classes select state or structure such as :focus-visible, :checked, :disabled, and :nth-child, while pseudo-elements represent generated boxes. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Hover is unavailable to many users, generated content may not be reliably exposed, and structural indexes count actual sibling rules. This draft therefore rejects: If an interface works on :hover, it automatically works for keyboard and touch users.

includes: Style interaction, structural and generated parts while preserving native behavior.
Implement hover, focus-visible, validation and structural states without hiding keyboard focus.
excludes: Hover is unavailable to many users, generated content may not be reliably exposed, and structural indexes count actual sibling rules.

outcomes: Implement hover, focus-visible, validation and structural states without hiding keyboard focus.

examples.title: Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements: concrete case
examples.situation: A control has visible :focus-visible styling independent of its hover styling.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A control has visible :focus-visible styling independent of its hover styling.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Design hover, focus, active, disabled, and touch behavior separately, with a visible :focus-visible state.
examples.result: The result follows because Design hover, focus, active, disabled, and touch behavior separately, with a visible :focus-visible state.

examples.title: Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements: boundary case
examples.situation: Hover is unavailable to many users, generated content may not be reliably exposed, and structural indexes count actual sibling rules.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: If an interface works on :hover, it automatically works for keyboard and touch users.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Design hover, focus, active, disabled, and touch behavior separately, with a visible :focus-visible state.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If an interface works on :hover, it automatically works for keyboard and touch users.
misconceptions.correction: Design hover, focus, active, disabled, and touch behavior separately, with a visible :focus-visible state.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: If an interface works on :hover, it automatically works for keyboard and touch users.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
